title: Task Scheduler (max-heap greedy)
code: public int LeastInterval(char[] tasks, int n)
foreach (int c in count) if (c > 0) heap.Enqueue(c, c);
foreach (int c in carry) heap.Enqueue(c, c);

frames.caption: Count each task: A:3, B:3. Cooldown n=2 means two identical tasks need ≥2 slots between them, so a cycle is n+1=3 slots.
frames.note: Count how many times each task must run, then put those counts in a MAX-heap so the most-frequent remaining task is always one O(1) peek away. The greedy insight: the bottleneck is whatever task appears most often, because copies of the SAME task must be spaced n apart. If we keep firing the highest-count tasks first we pack the schedule as tightly as possible and minimize idle slots.

frames.caption: Seed a MAX-heap with each task's count: [A:3, B:3]. The root is always the highest remaining count.
frames.note: Count how many times each task must run, then put those counts in a MAX-heap so the most-frequent remaining task is always one O(1) peek away. The greedy insight: the bottleneck is whatever task appears most often, because copies of the SAME task must be spaced n apart. If we keep firing the highest-count tasks first we pack the schedule as tightly as possible and minimize idle slots.

frames.caption: Cycle 1: fill up to 3 slots. Heap has [A:3, B:3].
frames.note: Process the schedule in cycles of exactly n+1 slots — the cooldown window, the minimum gap before a task may repeat. Within one cycle we run DISTINCT tasks (each at most once), so no task can violate its own cooldown. We pop the n+1 highest-count tasks, run them, and stash the still-remaining ones to re-enqueue after the cycle. This is the greedy move applied window-by-window.

frames.caption: Slot 0: pop the max → run A (was 3, now 2). time=1.
frames.note: Pop the current maximum-count task off the heap and run it this slot. Taking the largest first is the greedy choice: the most-frequent task has the least slack, so giving it the earliest available slot in every cycle is what keeps the total length minimal. Decrement its remaining count — if it still has copies left it will go back into the heap for a later cycle.

frames.caption: Slot 1: pop the max → run B (was 3, now 2). time=2.
frames.note: Pop the current maximum-count task off the heap and run it this slot. Taking the largest first is the greedy choice: the most-frequent task has the least slack, so giving it the earliest available slot in every cycle is what keeps the total length minimal. Decrement its remaining count — if it still has copies left it will go back into the heap for a later cycle.

frames.caption: Slot 2: heap is empty but 2 task(s) still cooling → idle "_". time=3.
frames.note: The heap emptied before the cycle filled its n+1 slots, so the remaining slots in THIS cycle are forced idle ("_") — there is simply no task that has cooled down yet. Idle time is the price of the cooldown; the greedy ordering guarantees we pay the least possible. (A non-final cycle always pads to full length; only the very last cycle may end early.)

frames.caption: End of cycle 1: re-enqueue survivors [A:2, B:2] — their cooldown is now satisfied.
frames.note: After draining the cycle, push every task that still has remaining copies back into the max-heap. They have now waited a full n+1-slot window, so their cooldown is satisfied and they are eligible again next cycle. The heap re-sorts them, so the next cycle once again starts from the most-frequent survivor — the greedy choice repeats.

frames.caption: Cycle 2: fill up to 3 slots. Heap has [A:2, B:2].
frames.note: Process the schedule in cycles of exactly n+1 slots — the cooldown window, the minimum gap before a task may repeat. Within one cycle we run DISTINCT tasks (each at most once), so no task can violate its own cooldown. We pop the n+1 highest-count tasks, run them, and stash the still-remaining ones to re-enqueue after the cycle. This is the greedy move applied window-by-window.

frames.caption: Slot 0: pop the max → run A (was 2, now 1). time=4.
frames.note: Pop the current maximum-count task off the heap and run it this slot. Taking the largest first is the greedy choice: the most-frequent task has the least slack, so giving it the earliest available slot in every cycle is what keeps the total length minimal. Decrement its remaining count — if it still has copies left it will go back into the heap for a later cycle.

frames.caption: Slot 1: pop the max → run B (was 2, now 1). time=5.
frames.note: Pop the current maximum-count task off the heap and run it this slot. Taking the largest first is the greedy choice: the most-frequent task has the least slack, so giving it the earliest available slot in every cycle is what keeps the total length minimal. Decrement its remaining count — if it still has copies left it will go back into the heap for a later cycle.

frames.caption: Slot 2: heap is empty but 2 task(s) still cooling → idle "_". time=6.
frames.note: The heap emptied before the cycle filled its n+1 slots, so the remaining slots in THIS cycle are forced idle ("_") — there is simply no task that has cooled down yet. Idle time is the price of the cooldown; the greedy ordering guarantees we pay the least possible. (A non-final cycle always pads to full length; only the very last cycle may end early.)

frames.caption: End of cycle 2: re-enqueue survivors [A:1, B:1] — their cooldown is now satisfied.
frames.note: After draining the cycle, push every task that still has remaining copies back into the max-heap. They have now waited a full n+1-slot window, so their cooldown is satisfied and they are eligible again next cycle. The heap re-sorts them, so the next cycle once again starts from the most-frequent survivor — the greedy choice repeats.

frames.caption: Cycle 3: fill up to 3 slots. Heap has [A:1, B:1].
frames.note: Process the schedule in cycles of exactly n+1 slots — the cooldown window, the minimum gap before a task may repeat. Within one cycle we run DISTINCT tasks (each at most once), so no task can violate its own cooldown. We pop the n+1 highest-count tasks, run them, and stash the still-remaining ones to re-enqueue after the cycle. This is the greedy move applied window-by-window.

frames.caption: Slot 0: pop the max → run A (was 1, now 0). time=7.
frames.note: Pop the current maximum-count task off the heap and run it this slot. Taking the largest first is the greedy choice: the most-frequent task has the least slack, so giving it the earliest available slot in every cycle is what keeps the total length minimal. Decrement its remaining count — if it still has copies left it will go back into the heap for a later cycle.

frames.caption: Slot 1: pop the max → run B (was 1, now 0). time=8.
frames.note: Pop the current maximum-count task off the heap and run it this slot. Taking the largest first is the greedy choice: the most-frequent task has the least slack, so giving it the earliest available slot in every cycle is what keeps the total length minimal. Decrement its remaining count — if it still has copies left it will go back into the heap for a later cycle.

frames.caption: Slot 2: heap empty and nothing waiting → last cycle ends early (no trailing idle).
frames.note: On the FINAL cycle we stop the instant the heap empties and do NOT pad with trailing idles — there is nothing left to wait for, so those slots simply do not exist. That is why this run is 8, not 9: the last cycle contributes only its 2 real tasks (A,B), not a full 3. The answer equals the greedy schedule length: A B _ A B _ A B.

frames.caption: Heap empty. Schedule is A B _ A B _ A B — length 8. That is the answer to LeastInterval.
frames.note: On the FINAL cycle we stop the instant the heap empties and do NOT pad with trailing idles — there is nothing left to wait for, so those slots simply do not exist. That is why this run is 8, not 9: the last cycle contributes only its 2 real tasks (A,B), not a full 3. The answer equals the greedy schedule length: A B _ A B _ A B.
